prompt: In 2026, which buyer personas and departments have fresh budget to spend on AI tools, and what are they buying? Where are new budget lines appearing? Cite sources.
content:
**In 2026, enterprise AI spending is shifting from discretionary pilots and IT/innovation silos to governed, strategic, portfolio-based investments distributed across business functions, with a strong emphasis on measurable ROI, governance, infrastructure scalability, and production deployment.**[[1]](https://www.tredence.com/blog/ai-spending)[[1]](https://www.tredence.com/blog/ai-spending)

Worldwide AI spending is forecast to reach approximately $2.52 trillion (a 44% YoY increase), driven heavily by AI infrastructure (projected ~$1.37 trillion, including a 49% rise in AI-optimized servers), followed by services, software, and models. Much of this flows through incumbent vendors rather than standalone “moonshot” buys, with priorities on proven outcomes over experimentation.[[2]](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-1-15-gartner-says-worldwide-ai-spending-will-total-2-point-5-trillion-dollars-in-2026)[[2]](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-1-15-gartner-says-worldwide-ai-spending-will-total-2-point-5-trillion-dollars-in-2026)

### Key Departments and Buyer Personas with Fresh or Expanding Budgets
AI budgets are moving beyond central IT/innovation teams into line-of-business functions. Mature enterprises now allocate spend directly to departments for specialized tools, agents, and use cases.[[1]](https://www.tredence.com/blog/ai-spending)[[1]](https://www.tredence.com/blog/ai-spending)

- **Finance/CFOs and Finance Teams**: Significant fresh or increased allocations. Nearly 60% of CFOs plan 10%+ increases in finance-function AI spend (with another ~24% expecting 4–9% gains). They prioritize automation for reporting, forecasting, regulatory/compliance tasks, and cost control. Buyer persona: CFOs focused on ROI visibility, governance to curb sprawl, and shifting from pilots to scalable ops.[[3]](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-02-10-gartner-research-reveals-cfos-budget-plans-prioritize-grotwth-functions-tech-and-ai-in-2026)[[4]](https://www.lyzr.ai/blog/enterprise-ai-spending/)

- **Sales and Revenue Teams (e.g., Sales Leaders, Revenue Ops)**: Demand for AI SDRs (sales development reps/agents), deal nurturing, lead enrichment, account-based marketing (ABM) agents, and pipeline tools. These tie directly to revenue outcomes.[[4]](https://www.lyzr.ai/blog/enterprise-ai-spending/)[[4]](https://www.lyzr.ai/blog/enterprise-ai-spending/)

- **Customer Support/Service and Operations**: AI agents for triage, email/phone support, case generation, and workflow automation to boost efficiency and reduce manual work.[[4]](https://www.lyzr.ai/blog/enterprise-ai-spending/)

- **Marketing (CMOs/Marketing Teams)**: Budget shifts toward AI for content creation, social media agents, ABM, SEO/AEO optimization, dynamic personalization, and campaign orchestration. Some marketing budgets are reallocating toward intent/trust-focused AI channels.[[5]](https://www.chilipiper.com/post/marketing-budget-ai-agents)[[6]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/neilkpatel_everything-you-know-about-marketing-is-changing-activity-7425252503962873856-sv3A)[[4]](https://www.lyzr.ai/blog/enterprise-ai-spending/)

- **HR (Mixed but Present)**: Internal copilots, L&D agents, performance reviews, onboarding, hiring assistants, and employee experience tools. Overall HR budgets face some pullback due to AI-driven efficiencies, but targeted AI investments continue.[[3]](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-02-10-gartner-research-reveals-cfos-budget-plans-prioritize-grotwth-functions-tech-and-ai-in-2026)[[4]](https://www.lyzr.ai/blog/enterprise-ai-spending/)

- **Legal and Compliance**: Contract review/extraction systems and regulatory monitoring tools.[[4]](https://www.lyzr.ai/blog/enterprise-ai-spending/)[[4]](https://www.lyzr.ai/blog/enterprise-ai-spending/)

- **Engineering/IT/Dev Teams and CIOs/CTOs**: AI tooling budgets (often 1–3%+ of engineering spend or specific carve-outs) for code authoring/review/debugging/security, plus broader infrastructure, agents, governance, and observability. CIOs receive more overall AI budget but face pressure for justification and ROI.[[7]](https://getdx.com/blog/how-are-engineering-leaders-approaching-2026-ai-tooling-budget/)[[8]](https://www.cio.com/article/4099548/how-to-get-ai-agent-budgets-right-in-2026.html)

- **Other Functions (e.g., Supply Chain, Procurement, Operations)**: Specialized automation and decision-support tools.[[1]](https://www.tredence.com/blog/ai-spending)

**Buyer personas** generally include department heads and functional leaders seeking productivity/efficiency gains (top reported benefit), cost reduction, revenue growth (aspirational for many), and customer experience improvements. Decision-makers emphasize governance, risk/compliance, measurable ROI, and avoiding vendor sprawl or shadow IT. Senior leadership (CEOs/CFOs) is increasingly driving top-down prioritization of high-impact workflows.[[9]](https://www.pwc.com/us/en/tech-effect/ai-analytics/ai-predictions.html)[[10]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/enterprise-ai-trends-2026-how-leaders-xecfc)

### What They Are Buying and New/Emerging Budget Lines
Spending categories include:
- **Core Infrastructure and Platforms** — AI-optimized servers/hardware, cloud compute, data platforms, vector databases, and orchestration tools (largest and fastest-growing area).[[2]](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-1-15-gartner-says-worldwide-ai-spending-will-total-2-point-5-trillion-dollars-in-2026)[[1]](https://www.tredence.com/blog/ai-spending)
- **AI Software, Agents, and Applications** — AI agents/copilots (e.g., SDR agents, support agents, contract reviewers), domain-specific tools, and application development platforms. AI agent software spend is rising sharply.[[11]](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-05-05-gartner-says-autonomous-business-and-artificial-intelligence-layoffs-may-create-budget-room-but-do-not-deliver-returns)[[4]](https://www.lyzr.ai/blog/enterprise-ai-spending/)
- **Data, Analytics, and Governance** — Data acquisition/preparation/governance, observability/monitoring, audit trails, compliance, security (including AI cybersecurity), and responsible AI frameworks. New or expanded lines for lifecycle costs (retraining, maintenance) and risk management.[[1]](https://www.tredence.com/blog/ai-spending)[[12]](https://stellium.consulting/articles/news/2026-ai-trends/)
- **Talent, Training, and Services** — Hiring/skilling (data scientists, AI engineers, product managers), AI literacy programs, and external services/partnerships.[[1]](https://www.tredence.com/blog/ai-spending)
- **Operational and Revenue Tools** — Automation for reporting, document processing, procurement, customer ops, and sales intelligence.[[4]](https://www.lyzr.ai/blog/enterprise-ai-spending/)

**New or expanding budget lines** appear in AI governance/compliance/risk (no longer optional), production-scale infrastructure and monitoring (vs. pilots), centralized visibility tools for multi-vendor spend, and full-lifecycle funding (deployment through ongoing ops). Budgets are moving toward portfolio approaches with strategic alignment, innovation reserves separated from scale spend, and cost transparency.[[1]](https://www.tredence.com/blog/ai-spending)[[1]](https://www.tredence.com/blog/ai-spending)

Overall IT spending is rebounding (projected >$6 trillion globally), with AI as a key driver, but approvals favor initiatives with clear ROI, operational impact, and risk reduction.[[10]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/enterprise-ai-trends-2026-how-leaders-xecfc)[[13]](https://www.splunk.com/en_us/blog/learn/it-tech-spending.html)

**Sources** primarily include Gartner forecasts and surveys (e.g., AI spending projections, CFO budget plans), industry analyses from Tredence, Lyzr, Deloitte, PwC, NVIDIA/State of AI reports, and related coverage. Data reflects 2025–early 2026 surveys and forecasts applicable to 2026 planning. Actual allocations vary by industry, maturity, and region (e.g., stronger in tech/finance).
